1:
ἐν ἀρχῇ ἦν ὁ λόγος — The imperfect ēn (was) is deliberate: it asserts pre-existence, not origin. Gen 1:1 opens "in the beginning God created" — John opens "in the beginning [the Word] already was." The Logos (G3056) is a high-dispute term (dispute level 3): its semantic range spans spoken word, reason, cosmic principle, and divine utterance. The Evangelist draws on all these registers while pressing beyond them.

καὶ θεὸς ἦν ὁ λόγος — The predicate nominative theos appears without the article. This is not a diminishing of the claim ("a god") but a grammatical consequence of predicate position (Colwell's rule): the anarthrous predicate precedes the verb and emphasizes quality — the Word was of the divine nature. The three clauses of v.1 assert distinct propositions: (1) the Word pre-existed all creation; (2) he was in personal relation with God (pros ton theon, "with God" — a directional preposition implying face-to-face orientation); (3) he shared the divine nature.
2: οὗτος ἦν ἐν ἀρχῇ πρὸς τὸν θεόν — The demonstrative houtos ("this one") gathers all three assertions of v.1 into a single referent before the Evangelist moves to creation. The repetition is not redundant: from the vantage point that the Word is God, the prior statements that he was with God and pre-existent are now seen together as the full portrait of the pre-incarnate Son.
3:
πάντα δι᾽ αὐτοῦ ἐγένετο — The verb egeneto (came to be, G1096) marks created things: they began to exist. The contrast with ēn (was) in v.1 is exact. The Word was; all things came to be through him. The preposition dia (through) marks instrumental agency — he is the agent through whom the Father creates.

χωρὶς αὐτοῦ ἐγένετο οὐδὲ ἕν — The negative statement does not merely repeat the positive; it closes off every exception. Ouden (nothing) reinforced by hen (one) — not even one thing — leaves no category of created being outside his scope. The Evangelist states the same truth from both directions to prevent any reader from limiting the Word's creative role.
4:
ἐν αὐτῷ ζωὴ ἦν — Zōē (G2222, dispute level 2) in John consistently refers to divine life — the life that belongs to God as opposed to merely biological existence (bios). The preposition en autō marks the life as intrinsic to the Logos, not borrowed or bestowed.

καὶ ἡ ζωὴ ἦν τὸ φῶς τῶν ἀνθρώπων — The life of the Logos becomes the light of humans: phōs (G5457) carries the sense of illumination, revelation, and moral clarity. John does not explain the mechanism; he asserts the equation. The light is for humans (tōn anthrōpōn), not a cosmic abstraction.
5:
καὶ τὸ φῶς ἐν τῇ σκοτίᾳ φαίνει — The present tense phainei (shines) is not historical: it describes the ongoing state of the light. The Prologue speaks in timeless register here — before and after the Incarnation, the light shines.

καὶ ἡ σκοτία αὐτὸ οὐ κατέλαβεν — Katelaben is an aorist of katalambanō (G2638), a verb that carries two distinct meanings: (1) to seize/overcome, (2) to comprehend/apprehend. Both may be in play simultaneously. The darkness has not overpowered the light and has not grasped or understood it. The aorist reaches back across the whole prior history of darkness encountering the light.
6: ἐγένετο ἄνθρωπος ἀπεσταλμένος παρὰ θεοῦ — The shift from timeless metaphysical statement to historical narrative is marked by egeneto (there came/arose). John the Baptist is introduced as a sent human: apestalmenos (having been sent, divine passive) places him firmly in the category of commissioned agent, not self-appointed prophet. Onoma autō Iōannēs — the Hebrew idiom "name to him was John" signals a figure of designated role.
7: οὗτος ἦλθεν εἰς μαρτυρίαν — Martyria (G3141) and its cognates dominate the first chapter. The word belongs to legal and courtroom registers: testimony before judges. John's role is formally evidentiary. The purpose clause hina martyrēsē peri tou phōtos makes his testimony instrumental: so that through him (di᾽ autou) all might believe.
8: οὐκ ἦν ἐκεῖνος τὸ φῶς — The emphatic pronoun ekeinos (that one) and the negation ouk ēn together rule out John's identification with the light absolutely. The Evangelist states what John was not before stating what he was, ensuring no reader conflates the Witness with the Thing witnessed. This negation will recur at 1:20 when John himself denies being the Messiah.
9:
ἦν τὸ φῶς τὸ ἀληθινόν — Alēthinos (G228, "genuine/real") differs from alēthēs (factually true). The claim is not merely that this light is truthful but that it is the genuine article, the archetype of which all other illumination is derivative. Phōtizei panta anthrōpon — lights every person (not "all men" in a gender-exclusive sense; anthrōpon is generic human).

The participle erchomenon (coming) is syntactically ambiguous: it may modify phōs (the light coming into the world) or anthrōpon (every person coming into the world). Most interpreters attach it to the light, since John's Gospel consistently describes Jesus as "the one coming" into the world.
10:
ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ ἦν — The word kosmos appears three times in this verse, carrying three slightly different senses: (1) the created order where the Word was present, (2) the created order he made, (3) the human world that failed to recognize him. The repetition creates an ironic ladder: he made what he was in; what he made did not know its maker.

καὶ ὁ κόσμος αὐτὸν οὐκ ἔγνω — Egnō (aorist of ginōskō, G1097) means to recognize, to come to know through experience. The world did not recognize him — not merely cognitive failure but relational non-reception.
11: εἰς τὰ ἴδια ἦλθεν — Idia (neuter plural) denotes his own domain, his own things — the creation, and specifically Israel as his covenantal inheritance. οἱ ἴδιοι αὐτὸν οὐ παρέλαβον — Idioi (masculine plural) shifts to his own people. The verb paralabein means to receive alongside, to take to oneself. The failure is not ignorance but rejection — they encountered him and did not take him in.
12:
ἔδωκεν αὐτοῖς ἐξουσίαν — Exousia (G1849) is authority or right conferred, not merely ability. The right to become children of God is a granted status, not a latent potential. Teknon (G5043, child) is used throughout John rather than huios (son), which is reserved for Christ. The distinction preserves the uniqueness of the Son's relationship.

τοῖς πιστεύουσιν εἰς τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ — The present participle pisteuousin describes ongoing believing. Eis to onoma — believing into his name — is directional: not merely believing about him but directing trust toward him as a person.
13: οἳ οὐκ ἐξ αἱμάτων οὐδὲ ἐκ θελήματος σαρκὸς οὐδὲ ἐκ θελήματος ἀνδρὸς ἀλλ᾽ ἐκ θεοῦ ἐγεννήθησαν — Three negations pile up before the positive statement. Haimaton (blood, plural in Greek — the mingling of parental bloods as ancient biology conceived generation); thelēmatos sarkos (desire of the flesh — physical reproductive drive); thelēmatos andros (will of a husband — the decisional act of a male head of household). The passive egennēthēsan (were born) marks divine initiative: the subject of the action is God, not the believer. New birth is not self-generated.
14:
ὁ λόγος σὰρξ ἐγένετο — Sarx (G4561, dispute level 4) is the highest-contested term in the Johannine vocabulary. Here it does not carry the Pauline sense of sinful nature but denotes the full human condition: mortal, embodied, subject to weakness and death. The choice of sarx over anthrōpos (human) or sōma (body) presses the point of genuine material humanity as far as language allows.

καὶ ἐσκήνωσεν ἐν ἡμῖν — Eskēnōsen (tabernacled, from skēnē = tent) is a Septuagint resonance: the divine Shekinah dwelling in the tabernacle. The Evangelist presents the Incarnation as the climactic divine dwelling — not in curtained fabric but in flesh.

μονογενοῦς παρὰ πατρός — Monogenēs (G3439, only-begotten) carries both uniqueness (one of a kind) and relational weight (the bond between father and only son). Both senses are needed: the gift costs what only an only son costs. Plērēs charitos kai alētheias — full of grace and truth — may echo the LXX pairing of hesed and emet (covenant faithfulness and reliability), the covenant character of God in Exod 34:6.
15:
Ἰωάννης μαρτυρεῖ περὶ αὐτοῦ — The present tense martyrei (testifies) treats John's historical testimony as standing testimony: it still witnesses. κέκραγεν is a perfect of krazō (to cry out): John has cried out and that cry stands on record.

ὀπίσω μου ἐρχόμενος ἔμπροσθέν μου γέγονεν — The temporal inversion is deliberate: "the one coming after me has come to rank before me." The explanation follows: hoti prōtos mou ēn — because he was before me (in existence). The one who arrives later in history existed first in eternity. Prōtos here is temporal, not merely positional.
16:
ἐκ τοῦ πληρώματος αὐτοῦ — Plērōma (G4138, fullness) is not simply "abundance" but the complete totality of divine grace residing in the Logos. The preposition ek (from/out of) marks derivation: what believers receive is drawn from his inexhaustible supply.

χάριν ἀντὶ χάριτος — The preposition anti is the exegetical crux. It most naturally means "in place of" or "instead of" — one grace replaced by another, grace succeeding grace in sequence (as the Mosaic covenant's grace is superseded by the grace of Christ, developed in v.17). Less likely is the reading "grace upon grace" (accumulation), which requires anti to function like epi.
17: ὅτι ὁ νόμος διὰ Μωϋσέως ἐδόθη — The law was given (edothē, aorist passive): it came through Moses as instrument, divinely bestowed, complete in the past. ἡ χάρις καὶ ἡ ἀλήθεια διὰ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ ἐγένετο — Grace and truth came to be (egeneto, aorist of ginomai): they arrived through a person, in an event. This is the first use of "Jesus Christ" by name in the Gospel — the full messianic title at the moment of introducing what surpasses Moses.
18:
θεὸν οὐδεὶς ἑώρακεν πώποτε — The perfect heōraken (has seen) covers all history to the present: no one at any point has had a direct vision of God. The claim grounds what follows.

μονογενὴς θεός — The preferred reading of the earliest manuscripts (ℵ* and B) has monogenēs theos (only-begotten God) rather than monogenēs huios (only-begotten Son). The harder reading is textually stronger; copyists were more likely to harmonize toward "Son" (the familiar title) than to introduce "God." Both readings assert the same Christological claim; the difference is terminological, not doctrinal.

ὁ ὢν εἰς τὸν κόλπον τοῦ πατρός — Ho ōn (the one who is/being, present participle) asserts ongoing intimate relation: the Son is continually in the Father's bosom/side (kolpos — the position of reclining proximity at a meal, cf. 13:23). Exēgēsato — he has narrated, disclosed, explained: the source of the English "exegesis." The Son who is at the Father's side is the authoritative expounder of the Father's character.
19: καὶ αὕτη ἐστὶν ἡ μαρτυρία τοῦ Ἰωάννου — The formal testimony section begins: martyria (G3141) frames what follows as legal evidence. The Ioudaioi — here rendered "Jewish leaders" — sent a delegation of priests and Levites (hiereis kai Leuitas), the credentialed religious authorities, to conduct the inquiry.
20: ὡμολόγησεν καὶ οὐκ ἠρνήσατο — The double statement — "he confessed AND did not deny" — is emphatic beyond normal rhetoric. Homologein (to confess publicly, G3670) and arneomai (to deny, G720) form polar opposites; stating both positive and negative reinforces the completeness of John's unambiguous declaration. ὅτι οὐκ εἰμὶ ἐγὼ ὁ χριστός — The word order places egō (I) after the verb for emphasis: "I am not — I — the Christ."
21: Ἠλίας εἶ σύ — The questions move through eschatological categories: Elijah (expected to return before the Day of the Lord, Mal 4:5), then "the Prophet" (the Mosaic prophet of Deut 18:15). John denies all three identities. The Synoptic tradition (Matt 11:14) has Jesus identify John as Elijah "in the spirit"; John's Gospel records John's own denial, and both can be true: John is not Elijah literally returned but fulfills Elijah's role.
22: The interrogators have exhausted their categories and need a reportable answer. The question tis ei (who are you?) with the demand for a self-definition for those who sent them reflects the bureaucratic obligation of official emissaries to return with usable intelligence.
23: ἐγὼ φωνὴ βοῶντος ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ — John identifies himself as "a voice" (phōnē) not a name, a person, or a title. The citation is from Isa 40:3 (LXX): a disembodied voice, instrumental and self-effacing. Euthynate tēn hodon Kyriou — make straight the way of the Lord. The Evangelist applies the LXX Kyriou (of the Lord) to Jesus, a significant Christological move.
24: A parenthetical note: among those sent were Pharisees (ek tōn Pharisaiōn). This explains the sharper follow-up question in v.25 — Pharisees were particularly concerned with unauthorized ritual innovation.
25: τί οὖν βαπτίζεις εἰ σὺ οὐκ εἶ ὁ χριστός — The logical issue: baptism as a ritual of purification and initiation was understood in Second Temple Judaism as connected to eschatological authority. If John is none of the expected end-time figures, by what authority does he administer a new rite? The question anticipates the sign-demand in 2:18.
26: ἐγὼ βαπτίζω ἐν ὕδατι — En hydati (in/with water, dative of instrument). John's baptism is plainly stated as water-only and therefore limited. μέσος ὑμῶν ἕστηκεν ὃν ὑμεῖς οὐκ οἴδατε — The verb hestēken is a perfect of histēmi — "has taken his stand and stands." The one they do not know is already present among them, not a future arrival. The contrast between John's visible water-rite and the unrecognized standing one is sharp.
27: οὗ οὐκ εἰμὶ ἄξιος ἵνα λύσω αὐτοῦ τὸν ἱμάντα τοῦ ὑποδήματος — Loosening a sandal strap was considered below the dignity of a disciple in relation to his teacher (the Talmud forbade disciples from performing this service for their own rabbi). John places himself below even that level — not a disciple unworthy of the menial task, but one who claims no standing to perform it at all. Axios (G514) = worthy, deserving.
28: ἐν Βηθανίᾳ ἐγένετο πέραν τοῦ Ἰορδάνου — The Evangelist specifies Bethany beyond the Jordan — not the Bethany near Jerusalem where Lazarus lived (11:1). The geographic precision serves as eyewitness warrant and distinguishes the two locations.
29: ἴδε ὁ ἀμνὸς τοῦ θεοῦ ὁ αἴρων τὴν ἁμαρτίαν τοῦ κόσμου — Amnos (G286, lamb) is the rare LXX word used in Isa 53:7 for the Servant led to slaughter. John does not use the more common arnion; amnos carries sacrificial weight. Airōn is a present participle — ongoing taking away, not a single completed act. Hamartian is singular (the sin, not "sins"): the cosmic condition of humanity before God, not merely individual offenses. Tou kosmou — of the world — extends the scope beyond Israel.
30: John repeats his earlier paradox (cf. v.15): the one coming temporally after him has surpassed him because he pre-existed him (prōtos mou ēn). The repetition is not redundant — it recurs now that Jesus is visible, giving the identification claim a definite referent.
31: κἀγὼ οὐκ ᾔδειν αὐτόν — The pluperfect ēdein (I had not known him) is significant: John's prior ignorance underscores the divine revelation in v.33. His recognition of Jesus was not prior acquaintance but divine disclosure. The purpose of his water-baptism ministry was that Jesus might be revealed (phanerōthē) to Israel — an apostolic purpose, not a personal one.
32: ὅτι τεθέαμαι τὸ πνεῦμα καταβαῖνον ὡς περιστερὰν ἐξ οὐρανοῦ — The perfect tetheamai (I have seen and the seeing stands) makes this eyewitness testimony in the strong sense: John's vision remains his standing evidence. Memenēken ep᾽ auton — the Spirit has remained on him (perfect): a permanent indwelling, not a prophetic visitation that departs. This distinguishes Jesus from OT prophets on whom the Spirit came and went; the dove's resting is the sign.
33: οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ βαπτίζων ἐν πνεύματι ἁγίῳ — The identification formula houtos estin (this is the one) formally designates Jesus. The contrast is complete: John with water (en hydati, v.31), Jesus with Holy Spirit (en pneumati hagiō). Pneuma (G4151, dispute level 3) is here unambiguous: the divine Spirit (Holy Spirit, not wind or human spirit), identified by the article and hagion.
34: κἀγὼ ἑώρακα καὶ μεμαρτύρηκα ὅτι οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ — Both verbs are perfect: heōraka (I have seen) and memartyrēka (I have testified) — John's sight and testimony both stand. The formal declaration huios tou theou (Son of God) closes the Baptist's witness, anticipating Nathanael's confession in v.49 and the Gospel's climactic purpose statement at 20:31.
35: A brief transitional verse: tē epaurion (the following day) marks the Evangelist's careful day-by-day structure in ch.1. John is present again with two disciples (duo ek tōn mathētōn autou) — unnamed at this point; one will be identified as Andrew in v.40.
36: ἴδε ὁ ἀμνὸς τοῦ θεοῦ — John repeats his identification of Jesus as the Lamb of God, now without the extended clause about taking away sin. In context the compressed repetition functions as a signal to his disciples: look at him. The aorist emblepsas (having fixed his gaze) marks the deliberate, directed attention.
37: καὶ ἠκολούθησαν τῷ Ἰησοῦ — The two disciples heard (ēkousan) and followed (ēkolouthēsan). Both are aorists: they heard, they followed. The chain of testimony — John points, disciples follow — is the pattern for the spread of belief throughout ch.1.
38: τί ζητεῖτε — The first words Jesus speaks in this Gospel: "What do you seek?" The question recurs thematically at the arrest (18:4, 7). Rhabbi (G4461) is transliterated from Aramaic and then translated: didaskalos (teacher). The parenthetical translations (hermēneuetai) throughout ch.1 signal an audience unfamiliar with Palestinian Jewish terminology. Pou meneis — "where do you stay/abide?" — menō (abide, remain) is a key Johannine verb that will carry enormous theological weight in chs. 14–15.
39: ἔρχεσθε καὶ ὄψεσθε — Imperative and future: "Come and you will see." The invitation pattern repeats at v.46. Tēn hēmeran ekeinēn emeinen — they stayed with him that day. The hour notation — hōra ēn hōs dekatē — is approximately 4:00 p.m. by Roman reckoning (the tenth hour of daylight from 6 a.m.) or 10:00 a.m. by Jewish reckoning. The Evangelist's precision may reflect eyewitness memory.
40: Ἀνδρέας ὁ ἀδελφὸς Σίμωνος Πέτρου — Andrew is identified as Simon Peter's brother — a reversal of what a reader might expect, since Peter will be the more prominent figure. The Evangelist names Andrew first precisely because he is the one present in this scene. The other disciple (likely the Evangelist himself, though unnamed) is left anonymous.
41: εὑρίσκει οὗτος πρῶτον τὸν ἀδελφὸν τὸν ἴδιον Σίμωνα — Prōton (first, perhaps "the first thing he did") — Andrew immediately seeks his own brother. Heuriskeō (find) becomes a structural verb in the call narratives of this chapter. Εὑρήκαμεν τὸν Μεσσίαν — The perfect heurēkamen (we have found!) marks a discovery that stands. Messias is transliterated from Hebrew/Aramaic and interpreted as Christos — both mean "Anointed One."
42: σὺ κληθήσῃ Κηφᾶς — The name-change is a future passive: you will be called Cephas. Kēphas is Aramaic for rock; Petros is the Greek equivalent. The parenthetical note ho hermēneuetai Petros again signals the non-Palestinian audience. Jesus gives Simon a new identity on first meeting, before any discipleship is established — a sovereign act of naming, recalling the divine name-changes of Abram/Abraham and Jacob/Israel.
43: ἀκολούθει μοι — The only place in ch.1 where Jesus himself initiates a call with the direct imperative "Follow me." Philip is found (heuriskei) by Jesus, not the other way around. The brevity of the exchange — command only, no dialogue — is characteristic of Jesus's authority in the call narratives.
44: The geographic note — Bethsaida as home of Philip, Andrew, and Peter — is historically specific and serves both narrative (Philip would have known Andrew) and topographic purposes for an audience mapping the early discipleship community.
45: εὑρίσκει Φίλιππος τὸν Ναθαναήλ — Philip continues the chain: found → tells → invites. His summary of what they have found is notably comprehensive: hon egrapsen Mōysēs en tō nomō kai hoi prophētai — the one Moses wrote about in the Law and the prophets also wrote about. He identifies Jesus by Torah and prophetic witness before naming him: "Jesus of Nazareth, son of Joseph" — a fully human identification.
46: ἐκ Ναζαρὲτ δύναταί τι ἀγαθὸν εἶναι — Nathanael's response is a proverb or rhetorical question, not personal hostility. Nazareth appears nowhere in OT prophecy; it was an obscure village in Galilee, itself looked down upon by Judeans. Philip's response — erchou kai ide (come and see) — does not argue but invites direct encounter, paralleling Jesus's own invitation in v.39.
47: ἴδε ἀληθῶς Ἰσραηλίτης ἐν ᾧ δόλος οὐκ ἔστιν — Alēthōs (truly, genuinely); Israēlitēs — an Israelite in the full covenantal sense; dolos (guile, deceit, G1388) absent from him. The description echoes Ps 32:2 (no guile in his spirit) and implicitly contrasts Nathanael with Jacob/Israel, the original deceiver whose name he bears — a "true Israel" whose heart has no Jacob in it.
48: πρὸ τοῦ σε Φίλιππον φωνῆσαι ὄντα ὑπὸ τὴν συκῆν εἶδόν σε — The temporal marker pro tou + infinitive: before Philip called you, while you were under the fig tree, I saw you. The aorist eidon asserts a specific past vision. What Jesus saw under the fig tree is not explained; the significance is the knowledge itself — supernatural insight into a private moment. Fig trees could serve as places of study and prayer in rabbinic culture.
49: Ῥαββί, σὺ εἶ ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ, σὺ βασιλεὺς εἶ τοῦ Ἰσραήλ — Nathanael's confession doubles: huios tou theou (Son of God) and basileus tou Israēl (King of Israel). The pairing is messianic rather than ontological at this stage — "Son of God" in Ps 2:7 and 2 Sam 7:14 is a royal covenant title for the Davidic king. Both titles will gain deeper content as the Gospel unfolds.
50: ὅτι εἶπόν σοι ὅτι εἶδόν σε ὑποκάτω τῆς συκῆς, πιστεύεις — Jesus acknowledges that Nathanael believes on the basis of a single sign (the fig-tree knowledge). The future opsē (you will see, singular) promises greater things: the plural of disciples in v.51 anticipates that Nathanael's seeing will be shared. Faith based on a single supernatural disclosure will grow to sight of heaven opened.
51:
ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν — The double amēn is a distinctive Johannine marker: it appears 25 times in this Gospel (always doubled), compared to the single amēn in the Synoptics. The doubling intensifies the solemn authority: what follows is not speculative.

τοὺς ἀγγέλους τοῦ θεοῦ ἀναβαίνοντας καὶ καταβαίνοντας ἐπὶ τὸν υἱὸν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου — The vision echoes Jacob's ladder at Bethel (Gen 28:12), where angels ascended and descended on it (or "on him" in some readings). John applies the movement to the Son of Man: Jesus is the locus of divine-human traffic, the new Bethel ("house of God"), the place where heaven opens to earth. Huios tou anthrōpou (Son of Man) appears here for the first time in the Gospel; it will carry an eschatological and exaltation trajectory throughout.
